California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 15,
1994
Michael H. Miller
City Attorney
City of Arcadia
240 West Huntington Drive
P.O.
Box 60021
Arcadia,
California
91066—6021
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94-204
Dear Mr. Miller:
This is in response to your letter requesting formal written
advice
1 on behalf of Arcadia City Councilmember Barbara D.
Kuhn
regarding her responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) 2
OUESTION
May Councilmember Kuhn participate in a series of decisions
concerning various elements of a specific plan for the downtown
area of the City of Arcadia,
including decisions to implement,
revise,
or terminate the specific plan and to determine the
potential funding for the specific plan elements?
CONCLUSION
Generally,
Councilmember Kuhn may not participate in
consideration of the specific plan if decisions concerning the
plan will have a material financial effect on her economic
interests as discussed below.
1
Please note that formal written advice is the application of
the law to a particular set of facts.
(Section 83114; Regulation
18329(b) (7).
Therefore, the immunity provided by this advice
letter is limited to the specific facts contained in this letter.
2
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-204
Page
2
However, it is possible that the various elements of the
specific plan may be considered separately to determine if each
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember.
Therefore, if decisions concerning a specific aspect of the plan
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember’s
economic interests,
they may be segregated and decided without the
councilmember’s participation.
Thereafter, the councilmember may
participate in consideration of other components of the specific
plan so long as:
(1)
those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the
councilmember was disqualified,
and
(2)
those decisions will not
have a material financial effect on any of the councilmember’s
economic interests.
Once the above deliberations are complete, however,
the
councilmember would not be able to vote on the final adoption of
the plan if she is disqualified from participating in specific
aspects of the plan.
FACTS
Councilmember Barbara
D.
Kuhn is a member of the Arcadia City
Council.
The City of Arcadia has a population of approximately
50,000.
Arcadia City Councilmembers are elected at large.
The City of Arcadia is also the Arcadia Redevelopment Agency
(the ARA”).
Councilmember Kuhn is also a member of the ABA Board
which acts as the governing board of the city’s ABA.
Since 1992, the ABA has been considering a number of
potential actions concerning a specific plan, the “Downtown
Revitalization Project,” within the City of Arcadia.
There are
approximately 840 businesses in the specific plan area out of a
total of 5,000 businesses in the city.
The ABA will determine the general direction of the specific
plan at a joint meeting of the city council and the ABA on
July 19,
1994.
At the meeting, actions may be taken to implement
or revise specific elements of the plan or to terminate the
specific plan.
Potential funding for specific plan elements may
also be considered,
including possible tax increments, interest
earnings,
land sale proceeds,
or possibly an agency bond issue.
By virtue of a partnership,
Councilmember Kuhn’s husband is a
25—percent owner of an office building located in a commercial
zone currently within the specific plan area.
The partnership,
“125 Wheeler Avenue Partnership,” rents office space to various
business tenants,
including Mr.
Kuhn’s architect business.
Councilmember Kuhn is a self—employed realtor and shares office
space with Mr.
Kuhn.


Our File No.
A-94-204
Page
3
It is expected that the ARA will consider the following
elements concerning the specific plan:
1.
Options concerning public street and sidewalk
improvements which may potentially be within 300 feet of the
office building.
2.
Incentive options to attract businesses such as
restaurants and specialty shops to the downtown area which also
may potentially be within 300 feet of the office building.
3.
Parking and pedestrian access ways within 2,500 feet of
the office building.
4.
Various zoning and land use decisions reexamining the
existing commercial uses, densities, permitted uses, lot coverage,
height,
set back,
intensity of use,
design elements, architectural
design features,
and increased use of sidewalks,
signage,
and
parking.
5.
Commercial facade rehabilitation,
downtown marketing,
directional signage,
co—op parking, merchant technical assistance,
and special events.
6.
Development of vacant lots within 600 feet of the office
building.
7.
Construction of an extension of a light rail station
within 300 feet of the office building.
You have asked whether Councilmember Kuhn may participate in
these decisions.
Appraisal information indicates that the light
rail station will enhance the value of the office building and
will therefore have a financial effect.
In addition, the zoning—
land use decision would also have some financial effect on the
real property.
Finally, the specific plan generally could impact
the value of the office building in excess of $10,000.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
The Political Reform Act (the “Act”),
was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative in 1974.
The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to
ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, will
perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a


Our File No.
A—94-204
Page
4
financial interest.
As a member of both the Arcadia City Council
and the City of Arcadia Downtown Revitalization Agency,
Councilmember Kuhn is a public official.
(Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Sections 87103(a)
—
(d).
According to your facts,
Councilmember Kuhn has the following
economic interests which may be foreseeably and materially
affected by the city council and ARA decisions.
1.
Real Property:
The councilmember’s husband is a 25—percent
owner of the commercial building within the specific plan area.
Presumably, his interest in real property is worth $1,000 or more.


Our File No.
A—94-204
Page
5
Pursuant to Section 87103,
an indirect interest of a public
official includes real property owned by an official’s spouse or
by a business entity in which the official’s spouse owns directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10—percent interest or greater.
Therefore,
the real property interest represents a potentially
disqualifying interest for Councilmember Kuhn for purposes of
Section 87103(b).
3
2.
Partnership:
An indirect interest of a public official also
includes an investment interest owned by an official’s spouse or
by a business entity in which the official’s spouse owns directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10—percent interest or greater.
The
councilmember’s spouse presumably has an investment interest in
the partnership which owns the office building in an amount
exceeding $1,000.
Therefore,
the councilmember has an indirect
interest in the partnership for purposes of Section 87103(a).
3.
spouse’s Income:
Income of an individual includes any
community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro
rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual or spouse owns directly,
indirectly,
or
beneficially a 10—percent interest or greater.
(Section 82030.)
Consequently, the following sources of income to the
councilmember’s spouse are potentially disqualifying sources of
income of the councilmember for purposes of Section 87103(c):
a.
Spouse’s Business
——
Mr.
Kuhn’s business if it has been
a source of income to Mr.
Kuhn in an amount of $500 or more
(making the councilmember’s community property interest $250)
within the past
12 months.
b.
Clients of Spouse’s Business
——
Mr.
Kuhn’s clients if
the income from a client is $500
(making her community property
interest $250)
or more.
c.
Business Tenants
——
The business tenants if the income
Mr.
Kuhn receives from the partnership from rental income is $500
or more.
4.
Councilmeinber’s Business:
The councilmember also has her own
real estate business.
Her real estate business and clients of the
business who have been sources of income of $250 or more within
the past 12 months are also potentially disqualifying economic
interests of hers.
(See also,
Sections 82005 and 87103(d).)
Please note, where any one economic interest of the
councilmember will be foreseeably and materially affected by a
decision,
the councilmember may not participate in the decision.
This would be the case even where the property was held by
Mr.
Kuhn as separate property.
(Section 87103.)


Our File No.
A-94-204
Page
6
Foreseeablilty and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community
Development Commission
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
However, the councilinember is only required to disqualify
herself from the decisions if the foreseeable effect on the
councilmember’s economic interests is also material.
If the
councilmember’s economic interest is directly involved in a
decision, then Regulation 18702.. 1
(copy enclosed)
applies to
determine materiality.
If the councilmember’s economic interest
is not directly involved in the decision,
but is indirectly
affected by the decision,
or if the effect of the decision is not
material under Section 18702.1,
then it must be determined if the
effect is material under Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6
(copies also enclosed)
as discussed below.
1.
Real Property:
Changes in Permitted Uses Within the
Downtown Revitalization Prolect Area
The councilmember has an indirect ownership interest in real
property within the specific plan area.
Therefore,
Councilmember
Kuhn may not participate in y decision concerning the specific
plan if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property.
With respect to zoning decisions,
an official’s interest in
real property is directly involved in a governmental decision,
and
the official’s disqualification is required,
when the decision
involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation,
sale,
purchase,
or lease,
or inclusion in or exclusion from any city,
county, district,
or other local governmental subdivision,
of real
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehold interest)
of $1,000 or more,
or a similar
decision affecting such property.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (A).)
Thus,
under your facts, disqualification would be required.
However, the terms “zoning”
and “rezoning” refer to the act
of establishing or changing the zoning or land use designation on
the subject property,
but does not refer to an amendment of an
existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation
(such as
changes in the uses permitted,
or development standards applicable
within a particular zoning category)
which is applicable to all
other properties designated in that category throughout the city.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (E).)


Our File No.
A-94-204
Page
7
If this is the case, the decisions concerning the real
property would still need to be analyzed to determine if the real
property would be affected indirectly by the pending decisions.
For decisions which may affect an interest in real property
indirectly but which do not involve a subject property from which
distances may be measured, the appropriate standard for
determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A)
and
(B).
(Regulation 18702.3(c).)
This would be the case concerning
decisions which affect property located within the specific plan
area.
(Chamberlain Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-183.)
Accordingly, for example, with respect to decisions
regarding the zoning of the councilmember’s property, the effect
of a decision is material if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market
value of the real property in which the public official has an
interest,
or will affect the rental value of the property by
$1,000 or more in a twelve-month period.
You have stated in your letter that appraisal
4 information
indicates that the zoning—land use actions would have some
financial effect on the councilmember’s real property.
Thus, if
these decisions will affect her interests in the office building
in the amounts stated above there would be a material financial
effect on the councilmember’s real property interests.
2.
Real Property:
Street Improvements
Redevelopment decisions are generally deemed to have a
material financial effect on an official’s property.
(Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)(D).)
However, your questions concern specific plan
decisions within a redevelopment area.
We have advised that a
specific plan decision for a discrete portion of the redevelopment
area is not considered a “redevelopment decision” as set forth in
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (D)
or Regulation 18702.3(e).
(Hawkins
Advice Letter,
No. A-92-070.)
Thus, decisions on the specific
plan would be considered as independent decisions to determine if
there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an
official’s economic interests.
(Marston Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—
120; Rosenthal Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—406.)
Pursuant to Regulation 18702.3, the indirect effect of the
decision on an official’s real property is material if:
(1)
The real property in which the official
has an interest,
or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries)
of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
Please note that the factors included in Regulation 18702.3(d)
must be considered with respect to appraisal information.


Our File No. A-94-204
Page
8
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official’s real property interest.
(2)
The decision involves construction of,
or
improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm
drainage or similar facilities,
and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within aradius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
Regulation 18702.3(a).
The decisions concerning public street and sidewalk
improvements would be deemed material with respect to the
councilmember’s real property if the improvements were made within
300 feet of the office building and there would be any financial
effect on the property, or new or substantially improved services
to the property would result.
(Regulation 18702.3(a),
subdivisions
(1)
and (3).)
However,
you would need to determine
this for each decision on a case—by—case basis.
Thus,
she would
not be able to participate in these decisions if her real property
will be materially affected as set forth above.
3.
Real Property:
Other Specific Plan Decisions
Regulation 18702.3 also sets forth standards for determining
materiality with respect to other specific plan decisions such as
funding decisions for a specific plan element,
or decisions
pertaining to the light rail, beautification, parking, access
ways, business attraction,
and building construction, which
indirectly affect real property.
According to your facts, the light rail station will enhance
the value of the councilmember’s property.
Therefore, the
couricilmember would have to disqualify herself from participating
in decisions concerning construction of a light rail station
within 300 feet of the office building.


Our File No.
A—94--204
Page
9
With respect to other specific plan decisions,
you have
indicated that the general effect of the project could affect the
value of the property in excess of $10,000.
Thus, it would appear
that each of the decisions will have some financial effect on the
councilmember’s real property.
However,
we cannot determine
whether the effects of the various decisions will be material as
to a specific component of the plan.
We must leave this ultimate
factual determination to the councilmember.
5
4.
Other Economic Interests
Additionally,
Councilinember Kuhn also has other economic
interests which may be indirectly affected by the decisions.
You
should consult the applicable regulations discussed below to
determine whether the following economic interests are
disqualifying with respect to each decision.
1.
Business Entities:
The councilmember’s business, her
spouse’s architect business,
the business tenants,
and the
partnership are potentially disqualifying economic interests of
hers.
(Section 82005;
87103(c);
87103(d).)
The applicable
standards for determining materiality as to business entities is
Regulation 18702.2.
For all but the largest business entities,
subdivision
(g)
of Regulation 18702.2 would apply.
2.
Clients:
The clients of Mr.
Kuhn’s business and the clients
of the councilmember’s business are also potentially diqualifying
sources of income to the councilmember.
(Section 87103(c).)
They
are either individuals or business entities which would be
affected indirectly by the specific plan decisions.
When a
business entity is indirectly affected by a decision, the
applicable standards for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2 discussed above.
The appropriate standard for
determining materiality as to an individual is that of Regulation
18702.6.
Secimentation of Decisions
The councilmember may participate in some decisions relating
to the specific plan, if they are severable from decisions to
which disqualification applies.
Generally,
each decision must be
analyzed independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable
material financial effect on an official’s financial interest.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
We have,
for example, previously advised that large and
complex decisions may, under certain circumstances,
be divided
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)


Our File No.
A—94--204
Page
10
into separate decisions
6 so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still
participate as to other components in which the official has no
financial interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter,
No.
1—90—542;
Huffaker Advice Letter,
No. A-86-343.)
Thus,
for example, if a
decision concerned parking and pedestrian access ways within 2,500
feet from the nearest boundary of the office building,
and this
decision was not interrelated to decisions concerning other,
closer portions of the specific plan, the official could
participate if the decisions would not affect the value of the
building by $10,000 or more or will affect the rental value by
$1,000 or more in a 12-month period.
Where decisions concerning the specific plan may be
considered separately, the following procedure should be followed
to permit the councilmember’s participation:
1.
The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from the other
decisions;
2.
The decisions for which the councilmember is disqualified
must be considered first,
and a final decision reached without her
participation;
3.
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
specific plan in which the councilmember has a disqualifying
interest,
she may participate in the deliberations regarding other
portions of the specific plan,
so long as those deliberations do
not result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from
which she was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)
7
6
Under some circumstances,
a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be segmented.
(Miller Advice Letter,
No.
A-82-
119.)
For example, if a decision concerning one portion of the
specific plan could decide or alter a decision for which the
councilmember has a conflict of interest, the councilmnember would
be disqualified as to both decisions.
(Nord Advice Letter,
No. A-82-038;
Scher Advice Letter,
No. A-88-479.)
Further, where
two decisions are alternatives, the decisions are too interlin]ced
to be considered separately.
However, to the extent that decisions involve the adoption of a
specific plan,
we have advised that a public official would not be
able to vote on the final adoption of the plan where he or she is
disqualified from participating in specific aspects of the plan
since those aspects of the plan may materially affect her real
property interests in unusual ways.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) CD);
Lanzone Advice Letter,
No.
A-93—135.)


Our File No. A-94-204
Page 11
“Public Generally” Exception
Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
For the “public generally”
exception to apply,
a decision must affect the official’s
interests in substantially the same manner as it would affect a
significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
Therefore, the remaining issue is whether the specific plan
decisions for the Downtown Revitalization Project will affect the
councilmember in a manner that is distinguishable from the effect
on the publió generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.)
You have stated in your letter that there are approximately
5,000 businesses,
of which 840 are located within the specific
plan area.
Pursuant to Regulation 18703(a), the material
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official’s
economic interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally if both subdivisions
(a) (1)
and
(a) (2)
below
apply:
(1)
Significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
(C)
The decision will affect the
segment of persons identified in


Our File No.
A-94-204
Page
12
California Code of Regulations, Title 2,
Sections 18703.2; or,
(D)
The decision will affect a
segment of the population which does not
meet any of the standards in subdivisions
(a) (1) (A)
through
(a) (1) (C),
however,
due
to exceptional circumstances regarding
the decision, it is determined such
segment constitutes a significant segment
of the public generally.
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the
official’s economic interest in substantially
the same manner as it will affect the economic
interests of the segment identified in
subdivision
(a) (1)
of this regulation.
On the basis of your facts, it appears that only 17 percent
of the businesses in the city would be affected by a decision.
Therefore, it does not appear that a significant segment of the
public generally will be affected by the decision in substantially
the same mannerd as the councilmember and the “public generally”
exception would not apply.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
-
By:
Luisa Men haca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosures
8
Moreover,
even if a specific plan decision affected 50 percent
of all the businesses in the jurisdiction, the applicable
councilmember’s interest must be affected in substantially similar
manner as persons in the specific plan area in order for the
exception to apply.
(In re Gilimor
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
38.)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0z per page.

sections.question:
May Councilmember Kuhn participate in a series of decisions
concerning various elements of a specific plan for the downtown
area of the City of Arcadia,
including decisions to implement,
revise,
or terminate the specific plan and to determine the
potential funding for the specific plan elements?
sections.conclusion:
Generally,
Councilmember Kuhn may not participate in
consideration of the specific plan if decisions concerning the
plan will have a material financial effect on her economic
interests as discussed below.
1
Please note that formal written advice is the application of
the law to a particular set of facts.
(Section 83114; Regulation
18329(b) (7).
Therefore, the immunity provided by this advice
letter is limited to the specific facts contained in this letter.
2Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

•
(916) 322-5660


Our Page
2
However, it is possible that the various elements of the
specific plan may be considered separately to determine if each
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember.
Therefore, if decisions concerning a specific aspect of the plan
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember’s
economic interests,
they may be segregated and decided without the
councilmember’s participation.
Thereafter, the councilmember may
participate in consideration of other components of the specific
plan so long as:
(1)
those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the
councilmember was disqualified,
and
(2)
those decisions will not
have a material financial effect on any of the councilmember’s
economic interests.
Once the above deliberations are complete, however,
the
councilmember would not be able to vote on the final adoption of
the plan if she is disqualified from participating in specific
aspects of the plan.
sections.facts:
Councilmember Barbara
D.
Kuhn is a member of the Arcadia City
Council.
The City of Arcadia has a population of approximately
50,000.
Arcadia City Councilmembers are elected at large.
The City of Arcadia is also the Arcadia Redevelopment Agency
(the ARA”).
Councilmember Kuhn is also a member of the ABA Board
which acts as the governing board of the city’s ABA.
Since 1992, the ABA has been considering a number of
potential actions concerning a specific plan, the “Downtown
Revitalization Project,” within the City of Arcadia.
There are
approximately 840 businesses in the specific plan area out of a
total of 5,000 businesses in the city.
The ABA will determine the general direction of the specific
plan at a joint meeting of the city council and the ABA on
July 19,
1994.
At the meeting, actions may be taken to implement
or revise specific elements of the plan or to terminate the
specific plan.
Potential funding for specific plan elements may
also be considered,
including possible tax increments, interest
earnings,
land sale proceeds,
or possibly an agency bond issue.
By virtue of a partnership,
Councilmember Kuhn’s husband is a
25—percent owner of an office building located in a commercial
zone currently within the specific plan area.
The partnership,
“125 Wheeler Avenue Partnership,” rents office space to various
business tenants,
including Mr.
Kuhn’s architect business.
Councilmember Kuhn is a self—employed realtor and shares office
space with Mr.
Kuhn.


Our Page
3
It is expected that the ARA will consider the following
elements concerning the specific plan:
1.
Options concerning public street and sidewalk
improvements which may potentially be within 300 feet of the
office building.
2.
Incentive options to attract businesses such as
restaurants and specialty shops to the downtown area which also
may potentially be within 300 feet of the office building.
3.
Parking and pedestrian access ways within 2,500 feet of
the office building.
4.
Various zoning and land use decisions reexamining the
existing commercial uses, densities, permitted uses, lot coverage,
height,
set back,
intensity of use,
design elements, architectural
design features,
and increased use of sidewalks,
signage,
and
parking.
5.
Commercial facade rehabilitation,
downtown marketing,
directional signage,
co—op parking, merchant technical assistance,
and special events.
6.
Development of vacant lots within 600 feet of the office
building.
7.
Construction of an extension of a light rail station
within 300 feet of the office building.
You have asked whether Councilmember Kuhn may participate in
these decisions.
Appraisal information indicates that the light
rail station will enhance the value of the office building and
will therefore have a financial effect.
In addition, the zoning—
land use decision would also have some financial effect on the
real property.
Finally, the specific plan generally could impact
the value of the office building in excess of $10,000.
sections.analysis:
Economic Interests
The Political Reform Act (the “Act”),
was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative in 1974.
The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to
ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, will
perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a


Our File No.
A—94-204
financial interest.
As a member of both the Arcadia City Council
and the City of Arcadia Downtown Revitalization Agency,
Councilmember Kuhn is a public official.
(Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Kuhn participate in a series of decisions
concerning various elements of a specific plan for the downtown
area of the City of Arcadia,
including decisions to implement,
revise,
or terminate the specific plan and to determine the
potential funding for the specific plan elements?

CONCLUSION: Generally,
Councilmember Kuhn may not participate in
consideration of the specific plan if decisions concerning the
plan will have a material financial effect on her economic
interests as discussed below.
1
Please note that formal written advice is the application of
the law to a particular set of facts.
(Section 83114; Regulation
18329(b) (7).
Therefore, the immunity provided by this advice
letter is limited to the specific facts contained in this letter.
2Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

•
(916) 322-5660


Our Page
2
However, it is possible that the various elements of the
specific plan may be considered separately to determine if each
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember.
Therefore, if decisions concerning a specific aspect of the plan
will have a material financial effect on the councilmember’s
economic interests,
they may be segregated and decided without the
councilmember’s participation.
Thereafter, the councilmember may
participate in consideration of other components of the specific
plan so long as:
(1)
those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the
councilmember was disqualified,
and
(2)
those decisions will not
have a material financial effect on any of the councilmember’s
economic interests.
Once the above deliberations are complete, however,
the
councilmember would not be able to vote on the final adoption of
the plan if she is disqualified from participating in specific
aspects of the plan.

Box 60021 Arcadia, California 91066—6021 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-94-204 Dear Mr. Miller: This is in response to your letter requesting formal written advice 1 on behalf of Arcadia City Councilmember Barbara D. Kuhn regarding her responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 2 OUESTION May Councilmember Kuhn participate in a series of decisions concerning various elements of a specific plan for the downtown area of the City of Arcadia, including decisions to implement, revise, or terminate the specific plan and to determine the potential funding for the specific plan elements? CONCLUSION Generally, Councilmember Kuhn may not participate in consideration of the specific plan if decisions concerning the plan will have a material financial effect on her economic interests as discussed below. 1 Please note that formal written advice is the application of the law to a particular set of facts. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(b) (7). Therefore, the immunity provided by this advice letter is limited to the specific facts contained in this letter. 2 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000 — 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-94-204 Page 2 However, it is possible that the various elements of the specific plan may be considered separately to determine if each will have a material financial effect on the councilmember. Therefore, if decisions concerning a specific aspect of the plan will have a material financial effect on the councilmember’s economic interests, they may be segregated and decided without the councilmember’s participation. Thereafter, the councilmember may participate in consideration of other components of the specific plan so long as: (1) those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the councilmember was disqualified, and (2) those decisions will not have a material financial effect on any of the councilmember’s economic interests. Once the above deliberations are complete, however, the councilmember would not be able to vote on the final adoption of the plan if she is disqualified from participating in specific aspects of the plan. The City of Arcadia is also the Arcadia Redevelopment Agency (the ARA”). Councilmember Kuhn is also a member of the ABA Board which acts as the governing board of the city’s ABA.
